subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: YCJA — Who is a Young Person
fact_pattern: A 17-year-old is arrested and charged with theft over $5,000. The question arises whether the Youth Criminal Justice Act applies to this accused.
call_of_question: Under s. 2(1) of the Youth Criminal Justice Act, who qualifies as a 'young person' subject to the Act?

options.A: A person who is 12 years of age or older but under 16 years of age at the time the alleged offence was committed.
options.B: A person who is 12 years of age or older but under 18 years of age at the time the alleged offence was committed.
options.C: A person who is 14 years of age or older but under 18 years of age at the time the alleged offence was committed.
options.D: A person who is under 18 years of age at the time of sentencing, regardless of age at the time of the offence.

explanation: Section 2(1) of the Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1 defines 'young person' as a person who is or was, while being proceeded against under the Act, twelve years of age or older but under eighteen years of age. The Act applies based on age at the time the alleged offence was committed, not at the time of trial or sentencing. The minimum age of criminal responsibility in Canada is 12 years — a child under 12 cannot be charged with a criminal offence. A 17-year-old at the time of the offence is a 'young person' within the YCJA.
why_A_wrong: The YCJA applies to persons 12 to under 18, not 12 to under 16. There is no upper age limit of 16 in the YCJA definition.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 2(1) of the YCJA, a young person is 12 years of age or older but under 18 years of age at the time the alleged offence was committed.
why_C_wrong: The minimum age under the YCJA is 12, not 14. A 12- or 13-year-old can be charged under the YCJA. The age range is 12 to under 18.
why_D_wrong: The relevant age is the age at the time the alleged offence was committed, not at the time of sentencing. If a person commits an offence while 17 but is sentenced at age 19, the YCJA still governs the proceedings (though adult sentences may be imposed in certain circumstances).

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Definition of Young Person — YCJA s. 2(1)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 2(1)

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 14-year-old is caught shoplifting for the first time. The value of the stolen goods was $30. The investigating police officer is considering how to handle the matter without a formal charge.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, which of the following best describes the framework for extra-judicial measures that a police officer may use in this situation?

options.A: A police officer has no authority to deal with a young person without laying a formal charge; all youth matters must proceed to court.
options.B: Extra-judicial measures are available only if the young person has no prior criminal record.
options.C: Extra-judicial measures — such as warnings, cautions, and referrals to programs — are available and are preferred over charges for first-time and minor offenders; a charge should be a last resort.
options.D: Extra-judicial measures require the consent of the victim before they can be used.

explanation: Sections 4-12 of the YCJA establish a robust framework of extra-judicial measures (EJMs) as the preferred response for young persons, particularly first-time and minor offenders. Section 4 states that extra-judicial measures are often the most appropriate and effective way to address young persons' offending behaviour without formal court proceedings. EJMs include police warnings, police cautions, Crown cautions, referrals to community programs, and extrajudicial sanctions (EJS) for more serious cases. Section 6 emphasizes that police should first consider whether an EJM is sufficient. The YCJA philosophy is that a charge should be a last resort, reserved for cases where EJMs are inadequate.
why_A_wrong: Police have broad authority to use EJMs without laying a formal charge. The YCJA expressly directs police to consider EJMs first (s. 6). A formal charge is a last resort, not the default.
why_B_wrong: EJMs are available even if the young person has a prior record, though a prior record may affect the appropriate type of EJM. The YCJA does not restrict EJMs to first-time offenders, though first-time status makes EJMs particularly appropriate.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Sections 4-12 of the YCJA establish EJMs as the preferred response for young persons, particularly first-time and minor offenders, with formal charges as a last resort.
why_D_wrong: Victim consent is not a general prerequisite for EJMs. Some EJMs (particularly extrajudicial sanctions involving victim-offender conferencing) may involve victim participation, but victim consent is not required for police warnings, cautions, or referrals.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Extra-Judicial Measures — YCJA s. 4-12
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 4-12

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Statement of a Young Person — YCJA s. 146
fact_pattern: A 15-year-old suspect is taken to the police station for questioning regarding an armed robbery. Before questioning begins, police explain the caution and the right to retain and instruct counsel. The youth says he understands but declines a lawyer. He then makes an inculpatory statement.
call_of_question: Under s. 146 of the YCJA, what additional step was required before police could take this young person's statement?

options.A: Police must have a parent or adult relative present during questioning, and if no parent is available, a responsible adult must be contacted.
options.B: No additional steps were required beyond the standard police caution and s. 10(b) Charter right to counsel.
options.C: Police must have obtained written consent from a parent or guardian before questioning a young person.
options.D: Police must advise the young person of the right to have a parent or other adult person present during questioning, and give the young person a reasonable opportunity to consult with that person.

explanation: Section 146(2) of the YCJA establishes specific requirements for a young person's statement to be admissible. Before the statement is made, the person who took the statement must have clearly explained to the young person, in language appropriate to their age and understanding: (a) the nature of the offence and the circumstances giving rise to it, (b) the right to retain and instruct counsel, (c) the right to have a parent or other appropriate person present, and (d) that the statement may be used as evidence. Section 146(2)(b) requires that the young person be given a reasonable opportunity to consult with counsel AND with a parent or other appropriate adult before making the statement. The failure to advise of and provide this opportunity is a basis to exclude the statement.
why_A_wrong: Section 146 does not require that a parent or adult actually be present during questioning. It requires that the young person be advised of the right to have such a person present and given a reasonable opportunity to consult. If the young person waives this right, the statement may still be admissible.
why_B_wrong: Additional steps beyond the standard caution and Charter right to counsel are required under s. 146 YCJA. The Act provides enhanced protections for young persons, including advisement of the right to have a parent or other adult present and the opportunity to consult with them.
why_C_wrong: Written consent from a parent or guardian is not required as a condition for taking a young person's statement under s.146. The requirement is advisement and reasonable opportunity to consult — the young person may waive parental presence. Written parental consent is not the statutory requirement.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 146(2) requires police to advise the young person of the right to have a parent or other adult person present and to give the young person a reasonable opportunity to consult with that person before the statement is made.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Statement of Young Person — YCJA s. 146
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 146(2)

tested_concepts: s. 146(2) YCJA requirements for youth statements
youth-specific protections beyond standard Charter caution

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 16-year-old is convicted of robbery under the YCJA. The sentencing judge is deciding among available youth sentences. Crown counsel submits that the appropriate sanction is a custodial youth sentence.
call_of_question: Under s. 39(1) of the YCJA, which of the following is the threshold requirement before a youth court may impose a custodial sentence?

options.A: The young person must have a prior record of at least one previous youth sentence.
options.B: The offence must be a designated violent offence as listed in the schedule to the YCJA.
options.C: The court must be satisfied that the offence is serious enough, the young person has a history that indicates a pattern of offending, or the exceptional circumstances of the case warrant custody; non-custodial sentences must have been considered inadequate.
options.D: The court requires the written consent of the youth's parents before imposing custody.

explanation: Section 39(1) of the YCJA restricts custodial sentences for young persons. A youth court shall not commit a young person to custody unless one of the following conditions is met: (a) the young person has committed a violent offence; (b) the young person has failed to comply with non-custodial sentences; (c) the young person has committed an indictable offence for which an adult would be liable to imprisonment for more than two years and has a history indicating a pattern of extrajudicial sanctions or non-custodial sentences; or (d) in exceptional cases where the young person has committed an indictable offence and the aggravating circumstances of the offence justify custody. Non-custodial sentences must have been considered and found inadequate before custody may be ordered (s.39(2)).
why_A_wrong: A prior record is not required before custodial sentences can be imposed. A young person with no prior record who commits a violent offence may receive custody under s.39(1)(a). Conversely, a prior record alone does not automatically lead to custody.
why_B_wrong: There is no 'scheduled' list of designated violent offences as a threshold for YCJA custody. The violence requirement is whether the offence is a 'violent offence' as defined in s.2 YCJA (causing, attempting to cause, or threatening to cause bodily harm), not a formal schedule.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 39(1) provides the threshold conditions for custodial youth sentences, and s.39(2) requires the court to consider non-custodial sentences as inadequate before imposing custody.
why_D_wrong: Parental consent is not required before a youth court may impose a custodial sentence. The YCJA governs the young person's sentence; parents participate in proceedings (with notification rights) but do not have a veto over sentencing outcomes.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Youth Custodial Sentence Threshold — YCJA s. 39
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 39(1), s. 39(2), s. 42

tested_concepts: non-custodial sentence must be considered inadequate

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 17-year-old is charged with first degree murder. The Crown applies for an adult sentence under the YCJA, arguing the youth sentence would not be sufficient to hold the accused accountable.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, which party bears the burden of proof on an adult sentence application, and what is the test the court applies?

options.A: The Crown bears the burden of proving that a youth sentence would not be adequate to hold the young person accountable, on a balance of probabilities.
options.B: The young person bears the burden of proving that a youth sentence is adequate, on a balance of probabilities.
options.C: The burden shifts to the young person once the Crown establishes a prima facie case for adult sentencing.
options.D: There is no burden of proof — the court makes the determination based on the pre-sentence report.

explanation: Under s. 72(1) of the YCJA (as amended), the youth justice court shall order that an adult sentence be imposed if it is satisfied that: (a) the presumption of diminished moral blameworthiness or culpability is rebutted; and (b) a youth sentence imposed under s.42 would not be of sufficient length to hold the young person accountable for their offending behaviour. The burden of proof on an adult sentence application falls on the Crown, who must prove, on a balance of probabilities, that a youth sentence would be inadequate. This reflects the YCJA's presumption that youth sentences are generally appropriate and that adult sentences are the exception.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 72(1) YCJA, the Crown bears the burden of proving, on a balance of probabilities, that a youth sentence would be inadequate to hold the young person accountable.
why_B_wrong: The burden is not on the young person to prove that a youth sentence is adequate. The YCJA presumes youth sentences are appropriate; the Crown must rebut this by proving adult sentencing is warranted.
why_C_wrong: There is no shifting burden mechanism in the YCJA adult sentencing framework. The Crown bears the burden throughout the application. There is no prima facie standard that triggers a shift.
why_D_wrong: The court does not operate without a burden of proof at an adult sentencing application. The determination is made on evidence and submissions, with the Crown bearing the balance of probabilities burden. The PSR informs the analysis but does not replace the burden of proof framework.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Adult Sentence Application — YCJA s. 64, s. 72
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 64, s. 72(1)

tested_concepts: YCJA presumption of youth sentence appropriateness

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Youth Records — Access and Sealing — YCJA s. 119-128
fact_pattern: Tariq was convicted at age 16 of theft under $5,000 and received a reprimand as his youth sentence. He is now 22 years old and has had no further contact with the justice system. A prospective employer conducts a criminal record check. Tariq's youth conviction was recorded in the RCMP repository.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, what is the status of Tariq's youth record at age 22, given that he received a reprimand and has been offence-free?

options.A: The youth record remains accessible indefinitely because it was entered in the RCMP national repository.
options.B: The youth record is sealed and inaccessible; the access period for a reprimand ends three months after the reprimand was pronounced.
options.C: The youth record is automatically destroyed two years after the sentence was completed.
options.D: The youth record can only be sealed if Tariq makes a formal court application after turning 18.

explanation: Under s. 119(2)(a) of the YCJA, where the young person received a reprimand as the youth sentence, the period during which persons are permitted access to records expires three months after the reprimand was pronounced. After this access period closes, the record is sealed and persons listed in s.119(1) can no longer access it. Since Tariq received a reprimand at age 16 and is now 22, the three-month access period has long expired. The record is effectively sealed. For more serious youth sentences, the access period is longer (e.g., three years after a non-custodial sentence is completed, five years after custody sentences end). The scheme reflects the YCJA principle that youth records should not follow young persons into adulthood.
why_A_wrong: Youth records in the RCMP repository are not permanently accessible. The YCJA establishes time-limited access periods that depend on the sentence imposed. A reprimand carries only a three-month access period under s.119(2)(a), after which the record is sealed.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s.119(2)(a) YCJA, the access period for a youth record where the sentence was a reprimand expires three months after the reprimand was pronounced. At age 22, Tariq's record is sealed.
why_C_wrong: The YCJA does not provide for automatic destruction of youth records two years after sentence completion. The scheme involves access periods that, once expired, result in sealing. Physical destruction may occur under s.128 in some circumstances but the two-year timeline stated is incorrect.
why_D_wrong: No formal court application is required to seal a youth record. Sealing is automatic once the access period expires under s.119(2). The young person need not apply to court — the record simply becomes inaccessible once the statutory period ends.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Youth Records — Access Periods — YCJA s. 119
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 119(2)(a), s. 128

tested_concepts: s. 119(2)(a) — three-month access period for reprimand
access period varies with sentence type

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Deferred Custody and Supervision Order — YCJA s. 42(2)(p)
fact_pattern: A 17-year-old is convicted under the YCJA of assault causing bodily harm, which is a violent offence. The sentencing judge is considering a deferred custody and supervision order (DCSO) as an alternative to immediate custody. Defence counsel argues that a DCSO would allow the accused to remain in the community while completing school.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, which of the following is a statutory prerequisite before the court may impose a deferred custody and supervision order?

options.A: A DCSO may only be imposed for non-violent offences; the violent nature of assault causing bodily harm categorically bars a DCSO.
options.B: A DCSO is available for any offence and requires only that the accused consent to the conditions.
options.C: A DCSO may be imposed only if the sentence that would otherwise be imposed is custody, and the court must be satisfied the young person's presence in the community would not create an undue risk to public safety.
options.D: A DCSO requires the Crown's consent and a supporting recommendation in the pre-sentence report.

explanation: A deferred custody and supervision order (DCSO) under s. 42(2)(p) of the YCJA allows a court to impose a period of community supervision in lieu of immediate custody. The conditions for a DCSO are set out in s. 42(5): (a) the young person is not found guilty of a presumptive offence or an offence for which an adult would be liable to more than two years' imprisonment would be inadequate; and (b) the court must be satisfied that the DCSO is consistent with the purpose and principles of youth sentencing and the young person's presence in the community on the terms and conditions proposed would not create an undue risk to public safety. The DCSO is functionally a suspended custody sentence — if breached, the young person can be brought back before the court and committed to custody. Assault causing bodily harm is not excluded categorically, though it is a violent offence that will require careful consideration of the undue risk criterion.
why_A_wrong: The YCJA does not categorically bar DCSOs for violent offences. Assault causing bodily harm is a violent offence, but the DCSO can still be considered if the undue risk to public safety criterion is satisfied. The bar relates to 'presumptive offences' (serious violent offences like murder and manslaughter) and offences carrying maximum adult sentences that make youth sentences inadequate.
why_B_wrong: A DCSO is not available for any offence simply upon accused consent. The court must independently be satisfied that the public safety criterion is met and that the DCSO is consistent with the purpose and principles of youth sentencing. Accused consent to conditions is part of implementation, not the threshold test.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 42(5) YCJA, a DCSO requires that custody would otherwise be the appropriate sentence and that the young person's presence in the community on the DCSO terms would not create an undue risk to public safety.
why_D_wrong: Crown consent is not a statutory prerequisite for imposing a DCSO. The sentencing judge may impose a DCSO without Crown consent, provided the statutory criteria are met. A PSR may be before the court but a recommendation favouring DCSO is not a precondition.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Deferred Custody and Supervision Order — YCJA s. 42(2)(p), s. 42(5)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 42(2)(p), s. 42(5)

tested_concepts: undue risk to public safety test

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 15-year-old pleads guilty to mischief causing damage of $3,000 to a community centre. The youth justice court judge wishes to involve the victim, parents, community members, and youth workers in developing an appropriate plan for the young person before sentencing.
call_of_question: Under s. 19 of the YCJA, which of the following best describes the conferencing mechanism the judge may use?

options.A: Conferencing under the YCJA is available only as an extra-judicial measure and cannot be ordered once a young person has pleaded guilty.
options.B: A youth justice court judge may convene or cause to be convened a conference for the purpose of obtaining advice on the appropriate youth sentence, including advice from the victim, parents, and community members.
options.C: Conferencing is mandatory whenever a young person pleads guilty to an offence causing property damage exceeding $1,000.
options.D: Conferencing requires the written consent of Crown counsel before the judge may order it.

explanation: Section 19 of the YCJA authorizes a youth justice court judge, a justice, a youth worker, or other persons to convene or cause to be convened a conference for the purpose of making decisions under the Act. A conference may be convened at any stage of proceedings — before or after a finding of guilt — and may be used to obtain advice on: appropriate extra-judicial measures, conditions for judicial interim release, sentence, and review of youth sentences. A conference may include the young person, the young person's parents or guardians, the victim, members of the extended family, community members, and youth justice professionals. It is a restorative justice mechanism consistent with the YCJA's rehabilitative philosophy. Participation is not mandatory for any party, and conferences do not replace the court's sentencing function.
why_A_wrong: Conferencing is not limited to the extra-judicial measures stage. Section 19 authorizes conferences at any stage of proceedings, including after a guilty plea. A post-guilty plea sentencing conference is expressly contemplated by the YCJA.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 19 YCJA, a youth justice court judge may convene or cause to be convened a conference, including for sentencing purposes, with participation from the victim, parents, community members, and others.
why_C_wrong: Conferencing is never mandatory under the YCJA. Section 19 gives judges discretion to convene conferences; it does not mandate them for any particular type of offence or damage threshold.
why_D_wrong: Crown consent is not a statutory prerequisite for ordering a conference under s. 19. The judge has independent authority to convene a conference without requiring Crown agreement. The Crown may participate in the conference but does not control whether one is convened.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Conferencing — YCJA s. 19
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 19

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Youth Records — Deemed Election and Publication Prohibition — YCJA s. 110
fact_pattern: A 16-year-old is charged with aggravated assault. A local newspaper reporter attends the youth court proceedings and wishes to publish a story identifying the young person by name, including a photo. The young person has not been sentenced to an adult sentence.
call_of_question: Under s. 110 of the YCJA, which of the following correctly states the default rule regarding publication of identifying information about a young person who is the subject of youth court proceedings?

options.A: Publication of identifying information about a young person is prohibited unless the young person consents and the court orders publication.
options.B: Publication of identifying information about a young person who has not received an adult sentence is prohibited; however, the young person may themselves apply to the court for an order permitting publication.
options.C: Publication of identifying information is prohibited as a default rule, but becomes automatically permitted once the young person turns 18.
options.D: There is no prohibition on publication because youth court proceedings are open to the public.

explanation: Section 110(1) of the YCJA prohibits any person from publishing the name of a young person, or any other information related to a young person, if it would identify the young person as a person dealt with under the Act. This prohibition applies to young persons who have not received an adult sentence. However, s. 110(2) and (3) create limited exceptions. Notably, s. 110(2)(b) provides that a young person who is 18 years of age or older may apply to the youth justice court for an order permitting publication. Also, publication may be permitted if a young person is at large and dangerous (s. 110(4)), or in certain other exceptional circumstances. The publication ban is a default rule reflecting the YCJA's principle of protecting young persons' privacy to facilitate rehabilitation.
why_A_wrong: The young person's consent alone is not sufficient to permit publication — court authorization under the YCJA's framework is required. The process is not simply consent-based; it involves a court order in specific circumstances set out in the Act.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 110(1) YCJA, publication of identifying information is prohibited by default. The young person (if 18 or older) may apply for a court order permitting publication under s. 110(2)(b), as one of the limited exceptions.
why_C_wrong: Turning 18 does not automatically permit publication. The publication ban continues after the young person turns 18. Exceptions require a court order or other specific circumstances set out in the YCJA, not merely the passage of time until adulthood.
why_D_wrong: Youth court proceedings may be open to the public but publication of identifying information is still prohibited under s. 110. Open court and publication bans can coexist — observers may attend, but publishing identifying information about the young person remains prohibited absent a court order or statutory exception.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Publication Prohibition — YCJA s. 110
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 110(1), s. 110(2)(b)

tested_concepts: young person may apply for publication order
open court vs publication ban distinction
